critic_raw:
GAN2Shape's central claim is that varying the StyleGAN2 latent vector w produces valid multi-view and multi-lighting observations of the same object, from which 3D geometry can be recovered. This is an extraordinarily strong implicit assumption about StyleGAN2's internal disentanglement that the methodology does not verify before building upon it.

StyleGAN2's latent space is trained to maximize image quality and diversity, not geometric consistency. There is no guarantee that trajectories in w-space correspond to actual viewpoint transformations: moving along w could produce a new face that merely looks like a rotated version rather than a geometrically consistent rotation of the same identity. The paper validates recovered 3D shapes visually (shape plausibility) rather than via quantitative geometric consistency metrics (e.g., multi-view reprojection error on held-out views, 3D chamfer distance against ground-truth scans). Evidence needed: a set of synthetic objects with known ground-truth 3D shapes, where w-space traversals are verified to produce metrically consistent viewpoint changes before the 3D recovery pipeline runs.

The method is evaluated on faces, cats, cars, and buildings — but StyleGAN2 models trained on these datasets have very different disentanglement properties. Human faces have the most structured latent space due to training data regularity; cars and buildings have weaker structural priors. The claimed generality across object categories is not supported by uniform evaluation methodology: if reconstruction quality varies substantially by category, it suggests the method is exploiting category-specific latent structure rather than a general principle.

The reliance on pre-trained StyleGAN2 weights creates an unacknowledged dependency on the specific GAN training regime. If the StyleGAN2 model underfits (e.g., was trained with limited data or fewer training steps), the latent space may lack the smooth structure necessary for the w-space traversal assumption to hold. The paper does not report sensitivity of recovered 3D quality to the quality of the underlying GAN.

Re-lighting and rotation are presented as downstream validation of recovered 3D shape, but both operations depend on an assumed lighting model (typically Lambertian). Non-Lambertian surfaces (metallic cars, fur on cats) violate this assumption systematically, producing plausible-looking but geometrically incorrect 3D estimates. The evaluation does not distinguish between cases where the lighting model is approximately valid versus cases where it is not.
all_issues_raised: StyleGAN2's latent space w is trained for image quality/diversity, not geometric consistency; moving along w may produce new-looking rotations rather than geometrically consistent viewpoints of the same object — this foundational assumption is verified only visually, not metrically.

The claimed generality across faces, cats, cars, and buildings is undermined because these object categories have very different latent-space regularity in StyleGAN2; stronger performance on faces may reflect face-specific disentanglement rather than a general mechanism.

The method's performance is undocumented with respect to the quality (training steps, dataset size) of the underlying StyleGAN2 model; a poorly-trained GAN would produce a non-smooth latent space that breaks the w-traversal assumption silently.

Re-lighting as downstream validation of recovered geometry assumes Lambertian reflectance; non-Lambertian surfaces (metallic cars, cat fur) produce plausible-looking but geometrically incorrect 3D estimates, and results are not conditioned on surface reflectance properties.
all_issues_adjudicated: StyleGAN2's latent space w is trained for image quality/diversity, not geometric consistency; moving along w may produce new-looking rotations rather than geometrically consistent viewpoints of the same object — this foundational assumption is verified only visually, not metrically.

The claimed generality across faces, cats, cars, and buildings is undermined because these object categories have very different latent-space regularity in StyleGAN2; stronger performance on faces may reflect face-specific disentanglement rather than a general mechanism.

The method's performance is undocumented with respect to the quality (training steps, dataset size) of the underlying StyleGAN2 model; a poorly-trained GAN would produce a non-smooth latent space that breaks the w-traversal assumption silently.

Re-lighting as downstream validation of recovered geometry assumes Lambertian reflectance; non-Lambertian surfaces (metallic cars, cat fur) produce plausible-looking but geometrically incorrect 3D estimates, and results are not conditioned on surface reflectance properties.
